turns.user: do you see theoretical advantage (or necessarity) of nonlinear FiLM ???
turns.assistant:
No. The Fourier time embedding already provides a nonlinear basis in t:

φ(t) = [sin(ω₁t), cos(ω₁t), ..., sin(ω_n t), cos(ω_n t)]

Linear FiLM on top of this gives:

γ(t) = W · φ(t) = Σₖ (aₖ sin(ωₖt) + bₖ cos(ωₖt))

This is already a rich nonlinear function of t — a truncated Fourier series. It can represent any smooth time dependence up to frequency ωₙ.

Adding a tanh hidden layer gives γ(t) = W₂ · tanh(W₁ · φ(t)), which can represent sharper/more complex time dependence. But there's no reason ...
turns.note: Theory-level question about expressivity of nonlinear vs linear FiLM; emphatic punctuation suggests skepticism.

turns.user: I am leaning  ln ρ  , since s = nabla phi , phi is a MLP
turns.assistant:
That's a clean idea. The advantages are real:

1. **Avoids the singularity**: φ = ln ρ → -∞ at nodes, but the MLP just outputs a large negative number. s = ∇_x φ via autodiff stays finite and smooth (bounded by MLP Lipschitz constant). No more trying to represent ±∞ in the network output.
